      Overview:
      - Within this thread you will find a detailed outline in regards to Combat Routine Development and creation. Each section will cover a crucial part needed within a Combat Routine or rather a part in which I feel is needed. Each section will also have a video guide in reference to the subject pretaining to the section you are studying in case the written examples I've given are not enough or hinder your grasp of the subject matter. Going forward this will serve as a guidline to aid in your creation and grant you insight so that, when you are finished, you'll have a creation you can be proud of.

      Requirements:
      - Current Honorbuddy release
      - Microsoft Visual Studios Express 2013 for Windows Desktop
      - World of Warcraft

      [HR][/HR]

      >> Update: 07/14/2014 <<

      Lesson ten complete!

      I want to make it very clear that this is not perfect and you will find errors. I will go back over and clean it up to reflect an exact replica of the video guide that will go along with this. Do not think that you can just copy and paste this into a .cs and it will work with flying colors. It was not intended for that and I did not set the guide up for this either. Take your time, learn the basics and get familiar with how it feels. Going forward we will be switching to CoRoutines but, until then this is our method. I hope you've enjoyed this guide thus far and I will have the videos, corrected information, and resources up for your viewing pleasure as soon as possible.      ​

      [HR][/HR]
      Materials:
      - Honorbuddy
      -       Microsoft Visual Studios Express 2013 for Windows Desktop
      -       World of Warcraft

      Resources:
      - This guide

      [      Video files go here]

    2. Dagradt Community Developer

      Joined:
      Jul 26, 2010
      Messages:
      1,423
      Likes Received:
      44
      Trophy Points:
      48
      Overview:
      -       In this section we will install, setup, and do a brief usage of the required tools needed to create your very own Combat Routine. I will not go into detail with this part of the guide as it is assumed you are savvy enough to do these tasks by yourself.

      Requirements:
      - Current Honorbuddy release
      - Microsoft Visual Studios Express 2013 for Windows Desktop

      [HR][/HR]

      >>       Lesson one: Tools of the Craftsman <<

      ​
      Tools are not just a means by which we create a work of art. Rather, they are the means by which we form our desires into a tangible item or entity to which we offer up to share with others. These tools used will only ever be as good as the user who guides them. In other words, you will need to refine your knowledge in each of the tools we will be using so that you will better understand the fluidity of what we will be accomplishing.

      There are two(2) main tools to which I will commonly refer back to, Honorbuddy & Visual Studios Express 2013 for Desktop. These tools will aid in your creation but, before you begin you must first learn the tools in which you will craft your work. At the bottom of this post is a materials list in which you will need to have installed and open before we begin. I've create a hyperlink attached to each name for your convenience so if you have not done so already, please click the respective links and install the required materials now. It is assumed that you already have World of Warcraft installed, thus it will not be present on the list at this time.

      *Please ensure that you have Visual Studios Express 2013 for Desktop up and running and the latest release of Honorbuddy installed before we begin. For the remainder of this guide I will be referring to Visual Studios Express 2013 for Desktop as "VS" and Honorbuddy as "HB".

      As of now, you should be versed enough to know the basics of how to create a new project and the simple usages within Visual Studios Express 2013 for Desktop assuming you used the references at the bottom of this post. It would be best if you selected a class and specialization to which you are familiar and comfortable with for this project. Seeing as I started out in the community as a Death Knight enthusiast, I will remain true to that calling and create a basic Combat Routine along those lines. So without further Adieu, lets get started.

      Within VS, please start a new project and name it whatever you feel like. Typically I name my projects after the class and specialization to which I will be creating but, be sure to select "Class Library" from the top!

      ​

      Now that we've gotten that out of the way, lets take a look at what we have thus far and attach HB to our project. On the right hand side, you will see a pane titled "Solution Explorer". It is here that you will navigate your project, add files and folders for future implementations, and link HB API to your creation. First lets add HB API to your project, you do this by right clicking on your project name from the solution explorer > Add > Reference. Currently you should see something similar to this:

      ​

      On the current screen you will need to add both "PresentationCore" and "System.Windows.Forms". Once done, select the "Browse" option and click the "Browse..." button at the bottom right. From this new pane select both "Honorbuddy.exe" and "Tripper.Tools.dll" and press "Add".

      ​

      Once back within VS, you should see the two(2) files you selected from HB inside the pane. Press "OK", solution explorer should now look similar to the following:

       ​

      Okay, we're getting there! Go ahead and click the solid white arrow to the left of "References" to close the listing and right click your project name, if you're confused it's the one right above "Properties". Now add the folders Core, Interface, and Rotation to your project. Once this has been done, add the following folders within your newly created folders:

      [Core]: Helpers, Managers, Utilities
      [      Interface]: GUI, Settings

      You should now look similar to the following:

      ​

      Getting all tingly and excited now, aren't you? Well don't be, your still a pathetic n00b and should be ashamed of yourself for needing me to baby you through this... Anyways, now we are going to be adding in the ".cs" files we will be needing throughout the rest of the project. The simplest way to do this is to select the folder in which you will be adding the file and press "Shift+Alt+C", of course if you have short stubby Gnome fingers or prefer the long method you can right click the folder in question > Add > Class. Whichever your method maybe, be sure to select the "Class" option at the top when entering the new class name. Now, please add the following .cs files to the respective folders:

      [      Core]: Spell.cs, Unit.cs
      [      Helpers]: Spell Book.cs
      [      Managers]: Hotkey Manager.cs, Talent Manager.cs
      [      Utilities]: Log.cs
      [      Rotation]: Conditions.cs, Rotation.cs

      You should now look something similar to this:

      ​

      Alright, now if you haven't done so already you should probably mash "Ctrl+S" to ensure that all is saved or click the save all button so all changes are recorded rather then just the current tab changes and with that you've just created the baseline for your project!

      [HR][/HR]
      Materials:
      -       Honorbuddy
      -       Microsoft Visual Studios Express 2013 for Windows Desktop

      Resources:
      -       How to Setup & Use Honorbuddy
      -       C# Beginners Tutorial
       
      Last edited: Jul 1, 2014
      Community Developer EST: 2010

      #2 Like Reply

    3. Dagradt Community Developer

      Joined:
      Jul 26, 2010
      Messages:
      1,423
      Likes Received:
      44
      Trophy Points:
      48
      Overview:
      -       In this section I will show you how to create the Main.cs file in which all base calls will derive from.Remember that it would be optimal if you chose a class a specialization that you're already familiar with and can test upon.

      Requirements:
      - Current Honorbuddy release
      - Microsoft Visual Studios Express 2013 for Windows Desktop
      - Hotkey Manager.cs file       optional
      - Rotation.cs file       optional
      - Unit.cs file       optional

      [HR][/HR]
      >> Lesson Two: Building the base <<​

      Welcome back! As of now, you should be all done with the initial setup of your project. In this lesson we will be looking at the "Main.cs" file and what is involved within. Now where did we leave off at... Oh, yes! You're a pathetic n00b still and should be only slightly less ashamed! Now then, currently among your folders you should see a "Class1.cs" file unless you've moved or renamed it. Double click this file and lets begin, you should see something similar to the below with the exception of your namespace:

      PHP:
      using System;
      using System.Collections.Generic;
      using System.Linq;
      using System.Text;
      using System.Threading.Tasks;


      namespace       Death_Knight_Frost__1H
      {
          public class       Class1
                {
          }
      }


      First off we're going to add a few markers in regards to who created the Combat Routine as well as some basic information about the class at hand. Add something like this to the very top, remember that "      //" means to comment out.

      PHP:
      //Created: 06/03/2014 by Dagradt
      //Professions:
      //Talents:
      //Glyphs:
      //Revision:


      Now that we have a little credit to yourself we can move forward to the basic declarations needed. First off you need to change the "public class Class1" to "public class Main", do this by right clicking the .cs file and rename it. After which add in the following statements:

      PHP:
      //Created: 06/03/2014 by Dagradt
      //Professions:
      //Talents:
      //Glyphs:
      //Revision:

      using Styx;
      using Styx.CommonBot.Routines;
      using Styx.WoWInternals.WoWObjects;
      using System;using System.Collections.Generic;
      using System.Linq;
      using System.Text;
      using System.Threading.Tasks;

      namespace       Death_Knight_Frost__1H
      {
          public class       Main : CombatRoutine
                {
              private static       readonly Version version = new Version(1, 0, 0);
              public       override string Name { get { return "Death Knight(Frost) 1H v" + version; } }
              public       override WoWClass Class { get { return WoWClass.DeathKnight; } }
              private static       LocalPlayer Me { get { return StyxWoW.Me; } }
          }
      }


      Now you're probably wondering what the hell you're doing and that's fine, you will need to watch the video guide that correlates to each of these lessons in order to hear the direct usage of particular functions and why. In a nutshell, the above statements can be explained as follows:

      private static readonly Version version = new Version(1, 0, 0);
      - This nothing more then a placeholder for an integer(your version)

      public override string Name { get { return "Death Knight(Frost) 1H v" + version; } }
      - This is what will appear as a name in the Combat Routine selection pane when you start HB

      public override WoWClass Class { get { return WoWClass.DeathKnight; } }
      - This tells HB as to what class the Combat Routine is subject to. Remember the Combat Routine selection screen that pops up after starting HB? This is what determines whether your Combat Routine will show up or not

      private static LocalPlayer Me { get { return StyxWoW.Me; } }
      - This allows us to simplify the usage of "StyxWoW.Me" to just "Me"

      Now most of you are probably confused and irritated right now because you can't get this to work correctly and VS keeps wanting to change what you're typing to something else entirely or you're getting red squiggly lines of doom all over the damn place! Relax and take a look at the declarations from when we started to how they look now.

      *Before:
      PHP:
      using System;
      using System.Collections.Generic;
      using System.Linq;
      using System.Text;
      using System.Threading.Tasks;


      *After:
      PHP:
      using Styx;
      using Styx.CommonBot.Routines;
      using Styx.WoWInternals.WoWObjects;
      using System;using System.Collections.Generic;
      using System.Linq;
      using System.Text;
      using System.Threading.Tasks;


      They look a little bit different don't they? Yours probably doesn't look anything like the "After" I've shown above and that's okay, let me explain. Lets go back to "public class Main : CombatRoutine", as you've noticed "CombatRoutine" has a red squiggly line underneath and is in white unless you know what you are doing of course. Click on the word and hover your mouse over the "Com" part, you should get a drop down menu. The first item on this this should be "using Styx.CommonBot.Routines;", select it and see what happens.

      "Oooooooooooooo..." That's me reading your mind and you're welcome!

      Now lets add our Precombat Buffs, Combat Buffs, and Combat calls to our file. By adding these statements to our project we are telling HB what to run during the specific event. Apply the following section below under the last line created in your project:

      PHP:
      #region [Method] - Implementations
      private Composite _preCombatBehavior, _combatBuffBehavior, _combatBehavior;
      public       override Composite PreCombatBuffBehavior { get { return _preCombatBehavior ?? (_preCombatBehavior = R.preCombatBuffing()); } }
      public       override Composite CombatBuffBehavior { get { return _combatBuffBehavior ?? (_combatBuffBehavior = R.combatBuffing()); } }
      public       override Composite CombatBehavior { get { return _combatBehavior ?? (_combatBehavior = R.rotationSelector()); } }
      #endregion


      The "#region" marker starts a collapsible area where as the "#endregion" ends the area. Adding the phrase "[Method] - Implementations" after the start marker applies a title to the collapsible area. Do not worry about the "R" before the function calls, we will fix this later on so let it error stand and lets continue to the remaining statements. We will now add in the "Initialization()" function. This in its simplest form declares a few things at HB startup, for the purpose of this guide, we do not need to get into it. Add in the following statement after the "public override Composite CombatBehavior { get { return _combatBehavior ?? (_combatBehavior = R.rotationSelector()); } }" line but, before "#endregion" of "[Method] - Implementations".

      PHP:
      #region [Method] - Hidden Overrides
      public override void Initialize()
      {
                Logging.Write(Colors.OrangeRed, "Hello {0}!", Me.Name);
                Logging.Write(Colors.White, "");
                Logging.Write(Colors.OrangeRed, "For optimal performance please use: Tyrael");
                Logging.Write(Colors.White, "");
                Logging.Write(Colors.OrangeRed, "Current Version:");
                Logging.Write(Colors.OrangeRed, "-- Frost 1H v" + version + " --");
                Logging.Write(Colors.OrangeRed, "-- by Dagradt --");
                Logging.Write(Colors.OrangeRed, "-- A Death Knight CoumbatRoutine --");
                HKM.registerHotKeys();
      }
      public       override bool WantButton { get { return true; } }
      public       override void OnButtonPress() { Logging.Write(Colors.OrangeRed, "Coming soon!"); }
      public       override void ShutDown() { HKM.removeHotkeys(); }
      #endregion

      Within the above statements we've done three(3) basic things.
      1) We've setup a simple post for HB so that once the Combat Routine is selected and HB has started, a post will greet the user and display the text as shown below:

      [picture here]

      2) We've setup the GUI or Settings button but, nothing more then "Coming Soon!" will show upon pressing such
      3) We've both registered the Hotkeys to which we will setup later and removed them upon stopping HB

      *Please note: Again do not worry about the "HKM", we will add this in later and the error will go away. Also, the "{0}" used with "Me.Name" allows use to replace "{0}" with the users toon's name! This can be used in many other ways as well.

      Lastly we will add the final section, "Pulse()", which does exactly what it says. Within "Pulse()" we will check only a few things as we do not want to bog down our Combat Routine and make it slow. Apply the last section below the "#endregion" of "[Method] - Hidden Overrides" but, again before the "#endregion" of "[Method] - Implementations".
      PHP:
      #region [Method] - Pulse
      public override void Pulse()
      {
          if (!      StyxWoW.IsInWorld || Me == null || !Me.IsValid || !Me.IsAlive)
              return;
          if (!      Me.Combat)
              return;
                U.Cache();
                U.enemyAnnex(30);
      }
      #endregion
      In the above we've created two(2) main checks along with two(2) functions to check for:

      if (!StyxWoW.IsInWorld || Me == null || !Me.IsValid || !Me.IsAlive)
      - This is used for non-combat placement, or checks to which we do not need to be in combat to check for

      if (!Me.Combat)
      - This is just the opposite of the above and checks to see if we are in combat or not

      U.Cache(); & U.enemyAnnex(30);
      - These are checks that we will discuss later on in the guide so leave them alone and we'll get to them soon enough

      *Please note: Don't worry about the "U"!

      Alright then, at this point you should look similar to this:
      PHP:
      //Created: 06/03/2014 by Dagradt
      //Professions:
      //Talents:
      //Glyphs:
      //Revision:

      using Styx;
      using Styx.Common;
      using Styx.CommonBot.Routines;
      using Styx.TreeSharp;
      using Styx.WoWInternals.WoWObjects;
      using System;
      using System.Collections.Generic;
      using System.Linq;
      using System.Text;
      using System.Threading.Tasks;
      using System.Windows.Media;

      namespace       Death_Knight_Frost__1H
      {
          public class       Main : CombatRoutine
                {        
              private static       readonly Version version = new Version(1, 0, 0);        
              public       override string Name { get { return "Death Knight(Frost) 1H v" + version; } }        
              public       override WoWClass Class { get { return WoWClass.DeathKnight; } }        
              private static       LocalPlayer Me { get { return StyxWoW.Me; } }

                    #region [Method] - Implementations        
                    private Composite _preCombatBehavior, _combatBuffBehavior, _combatBehavior;        
              public       override Composite PreCombatBuffBehavior { get { return _preCombatBehavior ?? (_preCombatBehavior = R.preCombatBuffing()); } }        
              public       override Composite CombatBuffBehavior { get { return _combatBuffBehavior ?? (_combatBuffBehavior = R.combatBuffing()); } }        
              public       override Composite CombatBehavior { get { return _combatBehavior ?? (_combatBehavior = R.rotationSelector()); } }

                    #region [Method] - Hidden Overrides        
                    public override void Initialize()       
              {
                        Logging.Write(Colors.OrangeRed, "Hello {0}!", Me.Name);            
                        Logging.Write(Colors.White, "");           
                        Logging.Write(Colors.OrangeRed, "For optimal performance please use: Tyrael");            
                        Logging.Write(Colors.White, "");            
                        Logging.Write(Colors.OrangeRed, "Current Version:");            
                        Logging.Write(Colors.OrangeRed, "-- Frost 1H v" + version + " --");            
                        Logging.Write(Colors.OrangeRed, "-- by Dagradt --");           
                        Logging.Write(Colors.OrangeRed, "-- A Death Knight CoumbatRoutine --");            
                        HKM.registerHotKeys();      
              }        
              public       override bool WantButton { get { return true; } }      
              public       override void OnButtonPress() { Logging.Write(Colors.OrangeRed, "Coming soon!"); }        
              public       override void ShutDown() { HKM.removeHotkeys(); }        
                    #endregion

              #region [Method] - Pulse        
                    public override void Pulse()        
              {            
                  if (!      StyxWoW.IsInWorld || Me == null || !Me.IsValid || !Me.IsAlive)                
                      return;           
                  if (!      Me.Combat)                
                      return;            
                        U.Cache();            
                        U.enemyAnnex(30);
              }        
                    #endregion

              #endregion    
                }
      }
      We're almost done! Just on last section to add, so lets get HKM, R, and U situated shall we? Add the following after "using System.Windows.Media;" but, before "namespace Death_Knight_Frost__1H", your namespace will be different so don't get confused!

      *Please note: keep in mind that this will not elevate the issue but, it will set us up later on so that we need not worry about them anymore. Also, make sure to change "Death_Knight_Frost__1H" to whatever your namespace is.
      PHP:
      #region [Method] - Class Redundancy
      using HKM = Death_Knight_Frost__1H.Core.Managers.Hotkey_Manager;
      using R = Death_Knight_Frost__1H.Rotation.Rotation;
      using U = Death_Knight_Frost__1H.Core.Unit;
      #endregion

      That's it! You're all done with the Main.cs file, please review below to make sure you are setup as I am for the following lessons:



      ​

      See, told you we'd fix some of those red squiggly lines!

      [HR][/HR]
      Materials:
      -       Honorbuddy
      -       Microsoft Visual Studios Express 2013 for Windows Desktop

      Resources:
      - This guide

      #3 Like Reply

    4. Dagradt Community Developer

      Joined:
      Jul 26, 2010
      Messages:
      1,423
      Likes Received:
      44
      Trophy Points:
      48
      Overview:
      - In this section I will show you how to create the Spell.cs file which contains the methods used to cast spells, detect spell cooldowns, and other similar methods commonly used within a Combat Routine.

      Requirements:
      - Current Honorbuddy release
      - Microsoft Visual Studios Express 2013 for Windows Desktop
      - Log.cs file optional

      [HR][/HR]
      >> Lesson three: Make It Rain <<​

      This will be a rather lengthy lesson so please do not rush through and ensure to read all of which is stated here within. First off, and not unlike all the others, lets start with out base "Spell.cs" file:

      PHP:
      using System.Collections.Generic;
      using System.Linq;
      using System.Text;
      using System.Threading.Tasks;

      namespace       Death_Knight_Frost__1H.Core
      {
          class       Spell
                {            
          }
      }
      There we are, simple! Now lets add in our regions:

      [      Cast Method]: Here we will add our methods that will tie into our Standard, Ground, and Non-Target casting functions
      [      Cooldown Detection]: Name says it all
      [      Ground Casting]: Function used to cast on the ground or unit
      [      Non-Target Casting]: Function used to cast a spell without the need for a target, whether it be yourself, opponent, or otherwise
      [      Standard Casting]: Do I really need to explain these..?

      PHP:
      using System;
      using System.Collections.Generic;
      using System.Linq;
      using System.Text;
      using System.Threading.Tasks;

      namespace       Death_Knight_Frost__1H.Core
      {
          class       Spell
                {
                    #region [Method] - Cast Method
              #endregion

              #region [Method] - Cooldown Detection
              #endregion

              #region [Method] - Ground Casting
              #endregion

              #region [Method] - Non-Target Casting
              #endregion

              #region [Method] - Standard Casting
              #endregion
                }
      }
      Now lets add in our declarations and don't forget to add in the references to get rid of those red squiggly lines!

      public delegate WoWUnit unitSelectionDelegate(object Context);
      - I honestly have no freaking clue what this is

      public delegate T Selection(object Context);
      - Nor this one either

      public static int lastSpellCast;
      - Placeholder to store a spell

      private static LocalPlayer Me { get { return StyxWoW.Me; } }
      - Allows us to simplify the usage of "StyxWoW.Me" to just "Me"

      private static WoWUnit currentTarget { get { return StyxWoW.Me.CurrentTarget; } }
      - Allows us to simplify the usage of "StyxWoW.Me.CurrentTarget" to just "currentTarget"

      PHP:
      using Styx;
      using Styx.WoWInternals.WoWObjects;
      using System;
      using System.Collections.Generic;
      using System.Linq;
      using System.Text;
      using System.Threading.Tasks;

      namespace       Death_Knight_Frost__1H.Core
      {
          class       Spell
                {
              public       delegate WoWUnit unitSelectionDelegate(object Context);
              public       delegate T Selection(object Context);
              public static       int lastSpellCast;
              private static       LocalPlayer Me { get { return StyxWoW.Me; } }
              private static       WoWUnit currentTarget { get { return StyxWoW.Me.CurrentTarget; } }

                    #region [Method] - Cast Method
              #endregion

              #region [Method] - Cooldown Detection
              #endregion

              #region [Method] - Ground Casting
              #endregion

              #region [Method] - Non-Target Casting
              #endregion

              #region [Method] - Standard Casting
              #endregion
                }
      }
      Lets attempt to make this as simple as possible by starting at the top region "Cast Method" and working our way down to the last "Standard Casting". Now, to start, we will add in the following three methods of which two are almost identical. Below you will find the code needed and should be placed within the "Cast Method" region:

      *You need to add "bool" between the "<>" bracket! My HTML is broken on the forum so if I add the correct code it messes everything up

      PHP:
      private static Composite castMethod(this int Spell, unitSelectionDelegate unitSelection, Selection<>reqs = null, bool gcdCast = false)
      {
          return new       Decorator(ret => ((reqs != null && reqs(ret)) || (reqs == null)) && unitSelection != null && unitSelection(ret) != null && SpellManager.CanCast(Spell, unitSelection(ret)),
              new       Action(ret =>
              {
                  if (      SpellManager.Cast(Spell, unitSelection(ret)))
                  {
                            lastSpellCast = Spell;
                            L.combatLog("Casting: " + WoWSpell.FromId(Spell).Name + " on " + unitSelection(ret).SafeName);
                      if (!      gcdCast)
                          return       RunStatus.Success;
                  }
                  return       RunStatus.Failure;
              }));
      }
      You can see here that we've created a "Composite" that requires three checks to be stated and a fourth that is set to "false" by default.

      *Again add "bool" between the "<>" brackets

      private static Composite castMethod(this int Spell, unitSelectionDelegate unitSelection, Selection<>reqs = null, bool gcdCast = false)
      - this int Spell refers to the spell that you want to cast
      - unitSelectionDelegate unitSelection refers to the target you wish to cast upon
      - Selection<>reqs = null refers to the checks you want to make and are set to null by default
      - bool gcdCast = false refers to an override to cast non-GCD spells off the GCD

      *Notice the difference between the above and below methods. The only difference here is that the below method does not require a target to cast, nor does it contain any of the target checks that the above method holds

      *Don't forget to add "bool" between the "<>" brackets!

      PHP:
      private static Composite castMethod(this int Spell, Selection<>reqs = null, bool gcdCast = false)
      {
          return new       Decorator(ret => ((reqs != null && reqs(ret)) || (reqs == null)) && SpellManager.CanCast(Spell),
              new       Action(ret =>
              {
                  if (      SpellManager.Cast(Spell))
                  {
                            lastSpellCast = Spell;
                            L.combatLog("Casting: " + WoWSpell.FromId(Spell).Name);
                      if (!      gcdCast)
                          return       RunStatus.Success;
                  }
                  return       RunStatus.Failure;
          }));
      }
      What the above two methods do is quite simple at its base. If you were to follow along starting at "return new Decorator" and with the exception that the second method does not check for a target, it would read like this:

      return new Decorator(ret => ((reqs != null && reqs(ret)) || (reqs == null)) && unitSelection != null && unitSelection(ret) != null && SpellManager.CanCast(Spell, unitSelection(ret)),
      - return this new check: if requirements are not null, requirements return within your specific range, your specified target is not null, your specified target's requirements are not null, and spell manager can cast your desired spell on your specified target

      new Action(ret =>
      - create new action

      if (SpellManager.Cast(Spell, unitSelection(ret)))
      - if spell manager was able to cast the desired spell on your specified target

      lastSpellCast = Spell;
      - placeholder is now equal to the spell name that you requested to be cast

      L.combatLog("Casting: " + WoWSpell.FromId(Spell).Name + " on " + unitSelection(ret).SafeName);
      - post "Casting: your desired spell name on your specified target's name"

      if (!gcdCast)
      - if gcdCast equals false

      return RunStatus.Success;
      - return as a success

      return RunStatus.Failure;
      - return as failed

      Okay then, now we are going to add the last method to this listing. This method allows us to cast reticle styled spells or placement spells:

      *Don't forget to add "bool" between the "<>" brackets!

      PHP:
      private static Composite dropMethod(this int Spell, unitSelectionDelegate unitSelection, Selection<>reqs = null, bool waitForSpell = false)
      {
          return new       Decorator(ret => unitSelection != null && reqs(ret) && SpellManager.CanCast(Spell) && (Me.Location.Distance(unitSelection(ret).Location) <= WoWSpell.FromId(Spell).MaxRange || WoWSpell.FromId(Spell).MaxRange == 0),
              new       Sequence(
                  new       Action(ret => SpellManager.Cast(Spell)),
                  new       DecoratorContinue(ctx => waitForSpell,
                      new       WaitContinue(1, ret => Me.CurrentPendingCursorSpell != null && Me.CurrentPendingCursorSpell.Id == Spell, new ActionAlwaysSucceed())),
                      new       Action(ret =>
                      {
                                SpellManager.ClickRemoteLocation(unitSelection(ret).Location);
                                lastSpellCast = Spell;
                                L.combatLog("Casting: " + Spell);
                      })));
      }
      I'll go over this later, tired and sleepy atm...

      Now we are going to simplify a few things here by preselecting certain information and creating a bridging function for the cast methods we've just made. Fill in the following regions as I have shown below:

      *Don't forget to add "bool" between the "<>" brackets!
      PHP:
      #region [Method] - Ground Casting
      public static Composite dropCast(this int Spell, unitSelectionDelegate unitSelection, Selection<> reqs = null)
      {
          return       dropMethod(Spell, unitSelection, reqs);
      }
      #endregion
      *Umm, well honestly this one really isn't simplified. We've just left out the "waitForSpell" bool on top and added in "true" down below and changed the function name

      *Don't forget to add "bool" between the "<>" brackets!
      PHP:
      #region [Method] - Non-Target Casting
      public static Composite blindCast(this int Spell, Selection<>reqs = null, bool gcdCast = false)
      {
          return       castMethod(Spell, reqs, gcdCast);
      }
      #endregion
      *Now this one takes into account the non-target method and once again we really didn't simplify anything other then giving it a more meaningful name

      *Don't forget to add "bool" between the "<>" brackets!

      PHP:
      #region [Method] - Standard Casting
      public static Composite Buff(this int Spell, Selection<>reqs = null, bool gcdCast = false)
      {
          return       castMethod(Spell, ret => Me, reqs, gcdCast);
      }

      public static       Composite Cast(this int Spell, Selection<>reqs = null, bool gcdCast = false)
      {
          return       castMethod(Spell, ret => currentTarget, reqs, gcdCast);
      }
      #endregion
      *Ha! Look, it's somewhat simplified. Here we've create a "Cast" and "Buff" function, selected the appropriate target, and left the gcdCast bool the same so... Yep, not really changing much here either

      These last three parts I am going to do quickly. First we're going to add some cooldown detection:

      PHP:
      public static double cooldownTimeLeft(this int Spell)
      {
          try
          {
                    SpellFindResults Results;
              return       SpellManager.FindSpell(Spell, out Results) ? (Results.Override != null ? Results.Override.CooldownTimeLeft.TotalMilliseconds : Results.Original.CooldownTimeLeft.TotalMilliseconds) : 9999;
          }
          catch (      Exception xException)
          {
                    L.diagnosticLog("Exception in cooldownTimeLeft(): ", xException);
              return       9999;
          }
      }
      Now add:

      *Don't forget to change your verbage

      PHP:
      #region [Method] - Class Redundancy
      using Action = Styx.TreeSharp.Action;
      using L = Template.Core.Utilities.Log;
      #endregion
      Lastly change "class Spell" to "static class Spell". That's it!
      *Ignore my green notes


      Materials:
      - Honorbuddy
      - Microsoft Visual Studios Express 2013 for Windows Desktop

      Resources:
      - This guide
       
      Last edited: Jul 8, 2014
      Community Developer EST: 2010
             PvP emphisized creations for your deathly pleasure to be reborn
      Do you like "      [Perdition's Reprieve]"? Click the star in the bottom left corner! Or keep me inspired with a donation @Paypal
      Dagradt, May 30, 2014 Report
      #4 Like Reply

    5. Dagradt Community Developer

      Joined:
      Jul 26, 2010
      Messages:
      1,423
      Likes Received:
      44
      Trophy Points:
      48
      Overview:
      -       In this section I will show you how to create the Unit.cs file in which both player and target detection methods reside. Also, this will house the aura detection methods used as well as caching.

      Requirements:
      - Current Honorbuddy release
      - Microsoft Visual Studios Express 2013 for Windows Desktop
      - Spell Book.cs file

      [HR][/HR]
      >> Lesson four: Let us scan <<​

      We're now going to start the "Unit.cs" section, so as always lets start with our blank file and add "static" before "class Unit":

      PHP:
      using Styx.WoWInternals.WoWObjects;
      using System;
      using System.Collections.Generic;
      using System.Linq;
      using System.Text;
      using System.Threading.Tasks;

      namespace       Death_Knight_Frost__1H.Core
      {
          static class       Unit
                {
          }
      }
      and like always lets add in our regions:
      PHP:
      using Styx.WoWInternals.WoWObjects;
      using System;
      using System.Collections.Generic;
      using System.Linq;
      using System.Text;
      using System.Threading.Tasks;

      namespace       Death_Knight_Frost__1H.Core
      {
          static class       Unit
                {
                    #region [Method] - Active Enemies
              #endregion

              #region [Method] - Aura Detection
              #endregion

              #region [Method] - Cache
              #endregion

              #region [Method] - Unit Evaluation
              #endregion
                }
      }

      Now we're going to start with the easiest sections first and work our way to the larger sections. First up we will take a look at a simple way to validate a target. This method will be used all over so we don't want it too large or cumbersome. Add the below code the to "Unit Evaluation" region:
      PHP:
      public static bool isUnitValid(this WoWUnit Unit, double Range)
      {
          return       Unit != null && Unit.IsValid && Unit.IsAlive && Unit.Attackable && Unit.DistanceSqr <= Range * Range;
      }

      The above code does a very simple check that will tell us if the target is question is, at it's essence, attack-able by us and the spell we wish to use. It checks for validity, dead or alive, attack-able, and distance, allowing us to ensure we use the correct spell on the target given it's current status.

      Oh, before I forget! Add this above the "Active Enemies" region:
      PHP:
      private static LocalPlayer Me { get { return StyxWoW.Me; } }
      private static       WoWUnit currentTarget { get { return StyxWoW.Me.CurrentTarget; } }
      Okay, now we're going to do some simple caching or whatever you'd like to call it. I don't do the new fancy methods you see as I don't need it for what I try to do. I only cache true or false and number variables that do not return a time calculation. Below is a simple posting of my cache section, you will see that I've used placeholders for you so that you may understand it better and I will be changing the names later as we get to it. Or maybe I'll just do it now..?

      PHP:
      public static bool auraOnMe;
      public static       bool auraOnTarget;
      public static       uint auraStackOnMe;
      public static       uint auraStackOnTarget;
      public static       uint mainResource;

      public static       void Cache()
      {
                auraOnMe = auraExists(Me, SB.auraName);
                auraOnTarget = auraExists(currentTarget, SB.auraName, true);
                auraStackOnMe = auraStacks(Me, SB.auraName);
                auraStackOnTarget = auraStacks(currentTarget, SB.auraName, true);
                mainResource = playerPower();
      }
      Now I'm only going to add those spells that are most crucial to the rotation as to not overwhelm our "Pulse()" function. This is what it should look like after the crucial changes have been made, notice how all returns are true/false or return a number without need for calculation:

      PHP:
      public static uint bloodCharge;
      public static       bool bloodPlague;
      public static       bool FrostFever;
      public static       bool killingMachine;
      public static       bool Rime;
      public static       uint runicPower;
              
      public static       void Cache()
      {
                killingMachine = auraExists(Me, SB.auraKillingMachine);
                Rime = auraExists(Me, SB.auraRime);
                bloodPlague = auraExists(currentTarget, SB.auraBloodPlague, true);
                FrostFever = auraExists(currentTarget, SB.auraFrostFever, true);
                bloodCharge = auraStacks(Me, SB.auraBloodCharge);
                runicPower = Me.CurrentRunicPower;
      }
      *Don't worry about the auraX stuff, we'll get to that next

      Next we will add the aura functions "Exists", "Stacks", and "TimeLeft". Each of these functions check for the same three things "Unit", "Spell", and "is it my spell" but, the information is calculated differently. Go ahead and add the following code sections to the "Aura Detection" region:

      PHP:
      public static bool auraExists(this WoWUnit Unit, int auraID, bool isMyAura = false)
      {
          try
          {
              if (      Unit == null || !Unit.IsValid)
                  return       false;
                    WoWAura Aura = isMyAura ? Unit.GetAllAuras().FirstOrDefault(A => A.SpellId == auraID && A.CreatorGuid == Me.Guid) : Unit.GetAllAuras().FirstOrDefault(A => A.SpellId == auraID);
              return       Aura != null;
          }
          catch (      Exception xException)
          {
                    L.diagnosticLog("Exception in auraExists(): ", xException);
              return       false;
          }
      }
      In the above we check for the target in which we are detecting from, the aura we want to check for, and if it is our aura or anyone's. The function then takes that information and determines whether or not the target has the aura in question.

      PHP:
      public static uint auraStacks(this WoWUnit Unit, int auraID, bool isMyAura = false)
      {
          try
          {
              if (      Unit == null || !Unit.IsValid)
                  return       0;                
                    WoWAura Aura = isMyAura ? Unit.GetAllAuras().FirstOrDefault(A => A.SpellId == auraID && A.CreatorGuid == Me.Guid) : Unit.GetAllAuras().FirstOrDefault(A => A.SpellId == auraID);
              return       Aura != null ? Aura.StackCount : 0;
          }
          catch (      Exception xException)
          }
                    L.diagnosticLog("Exception in auraStacks(): ", xException);
              return       0;
          }
      }
      So in the above we're doing the exact same thing except the information taken is calculated differently. Instead of checking solely for a true/false on if we have a aura we're instead check for the number of stacks an aura has and if we've the aura.

      PHP:
      public static double auraTimeLeft(this WoWUnit Unit, int auraID, bool isMyAura = false)
      {
          try
          {
              if (      Unit == null || !Unit.IsValid)
                  return       0;
                    WoWAura Aura = isMyAura ? Unit.GetAllAuras().FirstOrDefault(A => A.SpellId == auraID && A.CreatorGuid == Me.Guid) : Unit.GetAllAuras().FirstOrDefault(A => A.SpellId == auraID);
              return       Aura != null ? Aura.TimeLeft.TotalMilliseconds : 0;
          }
          catch (      Exception xException)
          {
                    L.diagnosticLog("Exception in auraTimeLeft(): ", xException);
              return       9999;
          }
      }
      Again with the above it's going to check if we have the aura on the specified target but, this time it is going to return the time left on the aura.

      Lastly we're going to take a brief look at the "Active Enemies" section and I mean brief as I do not use automatic switching for single target to AoE encounters. The user should know when to switch and should use the "aoeOn" key-bind to activate such. In any case here is a simple cache-like method that will store the number of active enemies within a given range on each pulse. It's slightly cumbersome so use at your own risk and I will not be using it any further within this guide:

      PHP:
      public static IEnumerable activeEnemies(WoWPoint fromLocation, double Range)
      {
          var       Hostile = enemyCount;
          return       Hostile != null ? Hostile.Where(x => x.Location.DistanceSqr(fromLocation) < Range * Range) : null;
      }

      private static List       enemyCount { get; set; }

      public static       void enemyAnnex(double Range)
      {
                enemyCount.Clear();
          foreach (var       u in surroundingEnemies())
          {
              if (      u == null || !u.IsValid)
                  continue;
              if (!      u.IsAlive || u.DistanceSqr > Range * Range)
                  continue;
              if (!      u.Attackable || !u.CanSelect)
                  continue;
              if (      u.IsFriendly)
                  continue;
              if (      u.IsNonCombatPet && u.IsCritter)
                  continue;
                    enemyCount.Add(u);
          }
      }

      private static       IEnumerable surroundingEnemies() { return ObjectManager.GetObjectsOfTypeFast(); }

      static       Unit() { enemyCount = new List(); }
      With the above code, your "Main.cs" file should now be free of errors. You will want to set your "U.enemyAnnex(x)", where "x" is the max distance of the spell(s) in which the "activeEnemies()" call is used.

      *Remember this from your Main.cs file? Just fill in for "x" where "x" is used

      PHP:
      #region [Method] - Pulse
      public override void Pulse()
      {
          if (!      StyxWoW.IsInWorld || Me == null || !Me.IsValid || !Me.IsAlive)
              return;
          if (!      Me.Combat)
              return;
                U.Cache();
                U.enemyAnnex(30);
      }
      #endregion
      Now then you should be looking like so:

      *Ignore my green thoughts and add in the redundancies per the picture below! Also I forgot to add static to the beginning so don't get confused






      Materials:
      -       Honorbuddy
      -       Microsoft Visual Studios Express 2013 for Windows Desktop

      Resources:
      - This guide
       
      Last edited: Jul 8, 2014
      Community Developer EST: 2010
             PvP emphisized creations for your deathly pleasure to be reborn
      Do you like "      [Perdition's Reprieve]"? Click the star in the bottom left corner! Or keep me inspired with a donation @Paypal
      Dagradt, May 30, 2014 Report
      #5 Like Reply

    6. Dagradt Community Developer

      Joined:
      Jul 26, 2010
      Messages:
      1,423
      Likes Received:
      44
      Trophy Points:
      48
      Overview:
      -       In this section I will show you how to create the Spell Book..cs file in which we will store our spell and aura identification numbers.

      Requirements:
      - Current Honorbuddy release
      - Microsoft Visual Studios Express 2013 for Windows Desktop
      - World of Warcraft
      - idTip AddOn

      [HR][/HR]
      >> Lesson five: The Book of Spells <<

      ​
      Hello again, in this section we will make a very simple means to list our needed spells and aura identification numbers for later use. Now before we begin I must make clear that, although using identification numbers is usually faster, it is not always the best practice to use the spell ID. In some cases the spell ID will not be recognized by HB and the spell's name will be needed, although this is a rare case indeed. To start you will need to navigate your solution explorer and find your "Spell Book.cs" file.

      * In case you don't remember where it is, it's located at Core > Helpers > Spell Book.cs

      To begin, you should be looking at something similar to the below code with the exception of your namespace:

      PHP:
      using System;
      using System.Collections.Generic;
      using System.Linq;
      using System.Text;
      using System.Threading.Tasks;

      namespace       Death_Knight_Frost__1H.Core.Helpers
      {    
          class       Spell_Book    
                {    
          }
      }
      Now we are going to add in two(2) regions, remember what regions are? Add in the following regions as I have:

      PHP:
      using System;
      using System.Collections.Generic;
      using System.Linq;
      using System.Text;
      using System.Threading.Tasks;

      namespace       Death_Knight_Frost__1H.Core.Helpers
      {    
          class       Spell_Book    
                {        
                    #region [Method] - Aura List        
              #endregion

              #region [Method] - Spell List        
              #endregion    
                }
      }
      Now, since we are using identification numbers instead of direct names we will need to use the "Constant" or "const" declaration as the identification numbers do not change when locked in. Now a glyph can change the identification number of a spell but, most likely you will not be changing your glyph usage during a fight or you can always add in a secondary ID for the glyph and spell combination. Add in the following declaration under the "public" type as we will be using these identification numbers outside of the file.

      *I'm not sure if I've stated this but, if you are calling or using the method outside of the file within another file you must set it to public or access from outside will cannot be made.
      PHP:
      using System;
      using System.Collections.Generic;
      using System.Linq;
      using System.Text;
      using System.Threading.Tasks;

      namespace       Death_Knight_Frost__1H.Core.Helpers
      {    
          class       Spell_Book    
                {        
              public const       int

                    #region [Method] - Aura List            
                        auraName = 0000,        
                    #endregion

              #region [Method] - Spell List            
                        spellName = 0000;        
                    #endregion    
                 }
      }

      For the moment we will leave the identification numbers at "0000" and the spell/aura names as they are as we'll tend to this in just a few moments. I've posted a link for you below that serves as a great means to easily get the identification number of a spell or aura. It's called "idTip" and is available at "Curse.com". Just install the AddOn and when you hover your mouse over a spell or aura, the identification number will appear at the bottom right of the tool tip.

      At this time you've completed the Spell Book.cs setup and should have something that looks similar to this:

      ​

      Okay, for ease of conveying the remaining section of this lesson I will be using the Death Knight one hand rotation from "SimulationCraft" as a base to determine which spell and aura identification numbers I will need throughout the Combat Routine. You can find the link to the site below in the references section, please note that these are tier 16 heroic rotations.

      Now what we are going to do is copy and past all of the rotations into Spell Book.cs and comment them out so that we don't get a million errors. Remember the usage of "//" for commenting out a section or if doing a bulk omission you can simply highlight the text and press "Ctrl+E" let go of this key combination and then press "C".

      *Please note: for undoing a bulk omission the same process can be used but, use "U" in lue of "C"

      Go ahead and copy in your rotation and don't forget to comment it out. Now you need to filter all the mess you have down to just the spell and aura names needed within the rotation, I trust you can do this on your own...

      Here's an example just in case you are still a pathetic fail of a n00b and your shame is at an epic level:

      //actions.single_target=blood_tap,if=talent.blood_tap.enabled&(buff.blood_charge.stack>10&(runic_power>76|(runic_power>=20&buff.killing_machine.react)))
      - Blood Tap is a spell
      - Blood Charge is an aura
      - Killing Machine is an aura
      - You need absolutely nothing else from this line

      *Make note that some calls will check for a spell and further down the line may call for the spell's aura, you'll want to make note of this so that you obtain both the spell and aura identification numbers which in most cases are the same number. However there are some cases where the identification numbers do not match.

      Once you've filtered all the spell and aura names needed, move them to their respective regions. You should look something similar to this:
      Now we're getting close to the finish line! Using the AddOn I mentioned earlier, add in the identification numbers per spell or aura into the format I gave earlier:

      auraName = 0000;
      spellName = 0000;

      Make sure that after each identification number you use "," but, make sure to use ";" after the very last one.

      *You might be wondering why I have the first word in lowercase and each following word capitalized, this is called camel case and is a standard code style.

      For reference, here is what you should be looking like once you're all done:
      That's it, you're all done with the Spell Book.cs file!

      [HR][/HR]
      Materials:
      -       Honorbuddy
      -       Microsoft Visual Studios Express 2013 for Windows Desktop
      -       World of Warcraft
      -       idTip AddOn

      Resources:
      -       SimulationCraft
       
      Last edited: Jun 30, 2014

      #6 Like Reply

    7. Dagradt Community Developer

      Joined:
      Jul 26, 2010
      Messages:
      1,423
      Likes Received:
      44
      Trophy Points:
      48
      Overview:
      -       In this section I will show you how to add basic key detection to your Combat Routine. This feature allows more functionality within your Combat Routine, letting the user decide when they want's something to happen without the need for a GUI.

      Requirements:
      - Current Honorbuddy release
      - Microsoft Visual Studios Express 2013 for Windows Desktop

      [HR][/HR]

      >>       Lesson six: Face roll like a pro <<

      ​
      Okay, so now we will go over some basic key detection code that will allow us to change actions upon a key press from the user. Like everything else we will start with our clean "Hotkey Manager.cs" file as shown below:
      PHP:
      using System;
      using System.Collections.Generic;
      using System.Linq;
      using System.Text;
      using System.Threading.Tasks;

      namespace       Death_Knight_Frost__1H.Core.Managers
      {
          class       Hotkey_Manager
                {
          }
      }
      Starting from this stage we will add in four bools in which we'll use as a placeholder to determine whether we are "on" or "off". Go ahead and add the following bools to your .cs file:

      PHP:
      public static bool aoeOn { get; set; }
      public static       bool cooldownsOn { get; set; }
      public static       bool manualOn { get; set; }
      public static       bool keysRegistered { get; set; }
      You should look like this now:

      *Please note: Each of these bools is a simple "get; set;", meaning get current state and set the bool to such

      PHP:
      using System;
      using System.Collections.Generic;
      using System.Linq;
      using System.Text;
      using System.Threading.Tasks;

      namespace       Death_Knight_Frost__1H.Core.Managers
      {
          class       Hotkey_Manager
                {
              public static       bool aoeOn { get; set; }
              public static       bool cooldownsOn { get; set; }
              public static       bool manualOn { get; set; }
              public static       bool keysRegistered { get; set; }
          }
      }
      Now create two regions and name as shown below:

      PHP:
      using System;
      using System.Collections.Generic;
      using System.Linq;
      using System.Text;
      using System.Threading.Tasks;

      namespace       Death_Knight_Frost__1H.Core.Managers
      {
          class       Hotkey_Manager
                {
              public static       bool aoeOn { get; set; }
              public static       bool cooldownsOn { get; set; }
              public static       bool manualOn { get; set; }
              public static       bool keysRegistered { get; set; }

                    #region [Method] - Hotkey Registration
              #endregion

              #region [Method] - Hotkey Removal
              #endregion
                }
      }
      Lets go ahead and fill in the "Hotkey Registration" region with the following code:

      PHP:
      public static void registerHotKeys()
      {
          if (      keysRegistered)
               return;
                HotkeysManager.Register("aoeOn", Keys.Q, ModifierKeys.Alt, ret =>
          {
                     aoeOn = !aoeOn;
                     Lua.DoString(aoeOn ? @"print('AoE Mode: \124cFF15E61C Enabled!')" : @"print('AoE Mode: \124cFFE61515 Disabled!')");
           });
                 HotkeysManager.Register("cooldownsOn", Keys.E, ModifierKeys.Alt, ret =>
           {
                     cooldownsOn = !cooldownsOn;
                     Lua.DoString(cooldownsOn ? @"print('Cooldowns: \124cFF15E61C Enabled!')" : @"print('Cooldowns: \124cFFE61515 Disabled!')");
           });
                 HotkeysManager.Register("manualOn", Keys.S, ModifierKeys.Alt, ret =>
           {
                     manualOn = !manualOn;
                     Lua.DoString(manualOn ? @"print('Manual Mode: \124cFF15E61C Enabled!')" : @"print('Manual Mode: \124cFFE61515 Disabled!')");
           });
                 keysRegistered = true;
                 Lua.DoString(@"print('Hotkeys: \124cFF15E61C Registered!')");
                 Logging.Write(Colors.OrangeRed, "Hotkeys: Registered!");
      }
      *Don't for get to clear your doom lines!

      The above code is simple and can be read as I've show below:

      if (keysRegistered)
      - If the keys have already been registered

      return;
      - Do not continue

      HotkeysManager.Register("manualOn", Keys.S, ModifierKeys.Alt, ret =>
      - This sets the "manualOn" key

      manualOn = !manualOn;
      - This resets the key to off

      Lua.DoString(manualOn ? @"print('Manual Mode: \124cFF15E61C Enabled!')" : @"print('Manual Mode: \124cFFE61515 Disabled!')");
      - This posts within the chat log in game the status of the key

      keysRegistered = true;
      - This sets the key registration to try so that we do not do it again less the bot is stopped so we don't reset randomly back to off

      Lua.DoString(@"print('Hotkeys: \124cFF15E61C Registered!')");
      - This posts within the chat log in game the status of the keys

      Logging.Write(Colors.OrangeRed, "Hotkeys: Registered!");
      - This posts within the HB console the status of the keys

      Now add this last section to the "Hotkey Removal" region:

      PHP:
      public static void removeHotkeys()
      {
          if (!      keysRegistered)
              return;
                HotkeysManager.Unregister("aoeOn");
                HotkeysManager.Unregister("cooldownsOn");
                HotkeysManager.Unregister("manualOn");
                aoeOn = false;
                cooldownsOn = false;
                manualOn = false;
                keysRegistered = false;
                Lua.DoString(@"print('Hotkeys: \124cFFE61515 Removed!')");
                Logging.Write(Colors.OrangeRed, "Hotkeys: Removed!");
      }
      Your red lines should be gone at this point and again this can be easily explained as follows:

      if (!keysRegistered)
      - If the keys are not registered

      return;
      - Do not continue

      HotkeysManager.Unregister("aoeOn");
      - Unregister the key

      aoeOn = false;
      - Set the key back to off

      keysRegistered = false;
      - Set key registration to off

      Lua.DoString(@"print('Hotkeys: \124cFFE61515 Removed!')");
      - This posts within the chat log in game the status of the keys

      Logging.Write(Colors.OrangeRed, "Hotkeys: Removed!");
      - This posts within the HB console the status of the keys

      Your finished product should be similar to this and although I have not gone over "press'n hold" key states, this should give you enough to get started. Review the section below to ensure you're accurate:

      PHP:
      using Styx.Common;
      using Styx.WoWInternals;
      using System;
      using System.Collections.Generic;
      using System.Linq;
      using System.Text;
      using System.Threading.Tasks;
      using System.Windows.Forms;
      using System.Windows.Media;

      namespace       Death_Knight_Frost__1H.Core.Managers
      {
          class       Hotkey_Manager
                {
              public static       bool aoeOn { get; set; }
              public static       bool cooldownsOn { get; set; }
              public static       bool manualOn { get; set; }
              public static       bool keysRegistered { get; set; }

                    #region [Method] - Hotkey Registration
                    public static void registerHotKeys()
              {
                  if (      keysRegistered)
                      return;
                        HotkeysManager.Register("aoeOn", Keys.Q, ModifierKeys.Alt, ret =>
                  {
                            aoeOn = !aoeOn;
                            Lua.DoString(aoeOn ? @"print('AoE Mode: \124cFF15E61C Enabled!')" : @"print('AoE Mode: \124cFFE61515 Disabled!')");
                  });
                        HotkeysManager.Register("cooldownsOn", Keys.E, ModifierKeys.Alt, ret =>
                  {
                            cooldownsOn = !cooldownsOn;
                            Lua.DoString(cooldownsOn ? @"print('Cooldowns: \124cFF15E61C Enabled!')" : @"print('Cooldowns: \124cFFE61515 Disabled!')");
                  });
                        HotkeysManager.Register("manualOn", Keys.S, ModifierKeys.Alt, ret =>
                  {
                            manualOn = !manualOn;
                            Lua.DoString(manualOn ? @"print('Manual Mode: \124cFF15E61C Enabled!')" : @"print('Manual Mode: \124cFFE61515 Disabled!')");
                  });
                        keysRegistered = true;
                        Lua.DoString(@"print('Hotkeys: \124cFF15E61C Registered!')");
                        Logging.Write(Colors.OrangeRed, "Hotkeys: Registered!");
              }
                    #endregion

              #region [Method] - Hotkey Removal
                    public static void removeHotkeys()
              {
                  if (!      keysRegistered)
                      return;
                        HotkeysManager.Unregister("aoeOn");
                        HotkeysManager.Unregister("cooldownsOn");
                        HotkeysManager.Unregister("manualOn");
                        aoeOn = false;
                        cooldownsOn = false;
                        manualOn = false;
                        keysRegistered = false;
                        Lua.DoString(@"print('Hotkeys: \124cFFE61515 Removed!')");
                        Logging.Write(Colors.OrangeRed, "Hotkeys: Removed!");
              }
                    #endregion
                }
      }



      [HR][/HR]
      Materials:
      - Honorbuddy
      -       Microsoft Visual Studios Express 2013 for Windows Desktop

      Resources:
      - This guide

    8. Dagradt Community Developer

      Joined:
      Jul 26, 2010
      Messages:
      1,423
      Likes Received:
      44
      Trophy Points:
      48
      Overview:
      -       In this section I will show you how to use user talent selections, remember I like simple!

      Requirements:
      - Current Honorbuddy release
      - Microsoft Visual Studios Express 2013 for Windows Desktop

      [HR][/HR]

      >> Lesson seven: Talents, yes it's that simple <<

      ​
      At this point you should all be familiar with navigating around the solution explorer and opening up a file within VS. The "Talent Manager.cs" file we will be working on is simple and like all the other files, you should see only the basic setup as shown below upon opening this file:
      PHP:
      using System;
      using System.Collections.Generic;
      using System.Linq;
      using System.Text;
      using System.Threading.Tasks;

      namespace       Death_Knight_Frost__1H.Core.Managers
      {    
          class       Talent_Manager    
                {    
          }
      }
      *Before we continue any further you need to remember that I do not use automated detection methods or any other automation functions when I do not need to or if it's already simple enough to do outside of a Combat Routine in the first place. With that being said, go ahead and add in the following statement as shown below:
      PHP:
      public static bool talentName = false;
      Yes, that's it. Currently this states that the talent is not selected but, usage of a "true" instead of "false" would state that the talent is selected by the user. Now, and again to make this easier, we will add in the talents that the rotation calls for us to check. Like we did in lesson five, we will use the rotation from "SimulationCraft" to figure out our talent checks.

      //actions.single_target=blood_tap,if=talent.blood_tap.enabled&(buff.blood_charge.stack>10&(runic_power>76|(runic_power>=20&buff.killing_machine.react)))
      - Here it is: talent.blood_tap.enabled

      Add in your talent names and set all to "false" for the time being. At this point you should look similar to this:

      You might be asking as to how the user will change their talent selections without needing to change the actual code and this is a fair enough question. Later on, during the GUI lessons, I will show you how to add in the options that will change the user's talent selections as they please via a GUI. So with that, you're done! On to the next lesson!

      [HR][/HR]
      Materials:
      - Honorbuddy
      -       Microsoft Visual Studios Express 2013 for Windows Desktop

      Resources:
      -       SimulationCraft

      #8 Like Reply

    9. Dagradt Community Developer

      Joined:
      Jul 26, 2010
      Messages:
      1,423
      Likes Received:
      44
      Trophy Points:
      48
      Overview:
      -       In this section I will show you how to create a simple logging method for your users to watch while they let your work do their bidding.

      Requirements:
      - Current Honorbuddy release
      - Microsoft Visual Studios Express 2013 for Windows Desktop

      [HR][/HR]

      >> Lesson eight: Spam it like it's hot! <<​

      Okay then, just as in lesson seven this will be short and sweet. Lets start with the basic setup:

      PHP:
      using System;
      using System.Collections.Generic;
      using System.Linq;
      using System.Text;
      using System.Threading.Tasks;

      namespace       Death_Knight_Frost__1H.Core.Utilities
      {    
          class       Log    
                {    
          }
      }
      Well that was simple enough, now lets add a placeholder to lock the current message and verify that we are not spamming the same message multiple times.

      *In case you didn't know, HB doesn't just cast the spell once and then be done with it. It spams the spell till it's detected that the spell has indeed been cast causing one hell of a message spam if you don't check for it.

      Add the following:

      PHP:
      public static string lastCombatMSG;
      This will serve as our placeholder to store the current message and allow us to verify that the messages are not duplicated. Lets add in the function now:
      PHP:
      public static void combatLog(string Message, params object[] args)        
      {            
          if (      Message == lastCombatMSG)                
              return;            
                Logging.Write(Colors.OrangeRed, "[Bacon] {0}", String.Format(Message, args));            
                lastCombatMSG = Message;        
      }
      What this function does is fairly simple:

      if (Message == lastCombatMSG)
      return;
      - If the current message is the same as the stored message, do nothing (actually it's being kicked out of the function)

      Logging.Write(Colors.OrangeRed, "[Bacon] {0}", String.Format(Message, args));
      - Post the current message in the following format (remember when I said we could replace "{0}" with various things?)

      lastCombatMSG = Message;
      - This new message will now be stored

      The question on your mind is probably, "where do I get the message from?". That will be derived from the Spell.cs file in the casting method we did in lesson three and don't forget to add in your declarations! You should be looking like this now:


      Now lets throw together the diagnostic log function. This one should be easy as you've just created one not a minute ago. Just follow the steps below:

      1) Copy and paste the current log function
      2) Change "lastCombatMSG" to "null"
      3) Change color "OrangeRed" to "Firebrick"
      4) Remove "[Bacon]"
      5) Remove "lastCombatMSG = Message;"

      *Your color selections may vary so keep that in mind!

      You should look similar to this:
      PHP:
      public static void diagnosticLog(string Message, params object[] args)
      {
          if (      Message == null)
              return;
                Logging.WriteDiagnostic(Colors.Firebrick, "{0}", String.Format(Message, args));
      }
      Now add in the following tags around the respective functions:
      PHP:
      #region [Method] - Combat Log
      #endregion

      #region [Method] - Diagnostics Log
      #endregion
      You should now be here:


      [HR][/HR]
      Materials:
      - Honorbuddy
      -       Microsoft Visual Studios Express 2013 for Windows Desktop

      Resources:
      - This guide
       
      Last edited: Jun 26, 2014
      Community Developer EST: 2010
             PvP emphisized creations for your deathly pleasure to be reborn
      Do you like "      [Perdition's Reprieve]"? Click the star in the bottom left corner! Or keep me inspired with a donation @Paypal
      Dagradt, May 30, 2014 Report
      #9 Like Reply

    10. Dagradt Community Developer

      Joined:
      Jul 26, 2010
      Messages:
      1,423
      Likes Received:
      44
      Trophy Points:
      48
      Overview:
      -       In this section I will show you how to implement spells and add checks so that you can maximize the output of your rotation.

      Requirements:
      - Current Honorbuddy release
      - Microsoft Visual Studios Express 2013 for Windows Desktop
      - Hotkey Manager.cs file
      - Spell.cs file
      - Spell Book.cs file
      - Unit.cs file

      [HR][/HR]

      >> Lesson nine: So... Should I cast it now? <<​

      Now if you have not yet finished lesson ten part one, do so now then come back to this lesson. Take your "Conditions.cs" file and using your "Rotation.cs" file we've created, add all spells to your "Conditions.cs" file and comment them out just like we did in lesson five.
      *Read through the remainder of this section BEFORE you begin!

      Now I'm not going to go over every single spell nor will I go into a lot of detail as this section can be skipped if you don't wish to use this method. The method found here will grant extra checks to your spells without needing to add them the the rotation checks. With this being said I will show you an example for each of the styles you will need to implement in order to do this section:

      [      Auto Attack]: This is standard for Melee classes only as Ranged classes, with the exception of Hunters, do not use this unless equipped with a wand
      PHP:
      #region [Method] - Auto Attack
      private static Composite autoAttack()
      {
          return new       Action(ret =>
          {
              if (!      Me.IsAutoAttacking && U.isUnitValid(currentTarget, 5))
                        Lua.DoString("StartAttack()");
              return       RunStatus.Failure;
          });
      }
      #endregion
      [Blind Cast]: This will cast a spell without the need for a target
      PHP:
      #region [Method] - Blood Tap
      public static Composite bloodTap()
      {
          return       S.blindCast(SB.spellBloodTap, ret => U.bloodCharge > 4 && (U.bloodRunes == 0 || U.frostRunes == 0 || U.unholyRunes == 0), true);
      }
      #endregion
      [Buff Cast]: This will cast a spell on you and only you
      PHP:
      #region [Method] - Horn of Winter
      public static Composite hornofWinter()
      {
          return       S.Buff(SB.spellHornofWinter, ret => true);
      }
      #endregion
      [Drop Cast]: This will cast a reticle spell on a given "Unit" location
      PHP:
      #region [Method] - Death and Decay
      public static Composite deathandDecay(WoWUnit Unit, double Range)
      {
          return       S.dropCast(SB.spellDeathandDecay, ret => Unit, ret => U.isUnitValid(Unit, Range));
      }
      #endregion
      [Standard Cast]: This will cast a spell on your current target
      PHP:
      #region [Method] - Obliterate
      public static Composite Obliterate(WoWUnit Unit, double Range)
      {
          return       S.Cast(SB.spellObliterate, ret => U.isUnitValid(Unit, Range));
      }
      #endregion

      Now, lets go over the above. With the exception of the "autoAttack()", "S.Buff()", and "S.blindCast()" functions, we will require two things in each of these "Composites". These two things are the "Unit" we wish to cast upon and the "Range" at which our spell will make contact. These two requirements can be removed from the beginning because, as you can see, inside the call we again require the same thing via the "U.isUnitValid(Unit, Range)" function which in turn is checked by our base function found in "Unit.cs". The use of these two requirements in the beginning of the "Composite" are nothing more then an easy, readable, and highly redundant means that I like to use. The reason I use this method overall is not to verify the "Unit" or "Range" but, rather to allow me to add extra checks to the spell that the "Rotation.cs" doesn't require. If you do not have a need to PvP checks or any other extra needs, you do not need this section and I will show you a simpler method of doing things in lesson ten.

      In case you are still confused at what I mean, here is an example. Let's take the "Obliterate" and "Frost Strike" spells and set them in a PvP environment such as an arena. Now I'm old school and I still find the old ways to be the best and most true. I rarely every use "Obliterate" on a "Plate" or "Mail" target. Why? Because "Plate" and "Mail" both negate "Physical" damage at there base. "Cloth" and "Leather" targets do not negate much of a "Physical" attack and as such I save my "Obliterate" for those targets to achieve a greater damage output. "Frost Strike" on the other hand is pure "Magic" damage which no armor really negates at it's base so I spam that everywhere. In this example I would say that I do not want to use "Obliterate" on a "Plate" target less he's almost dead and I can guarantee a kill with one or two hits. This extra check would be added to the call above and is outside of the standard rotational calls we'd normally use. So instead of creating a huge bulk check, I instead am checking for if the spells is best to use and then checking for the spell usage conditionals. Make sense now? Probably not...

      Materials:
      -       Honorbuddy
      -       Microsoft Visual Studios Express 2013 for Windows Desktop

      Resources:
      - This guide
